name: Jute
description: Jute (Corchorus spp.) is a major biodegradable bast fiber crop, widely known as the Golden Fibre and economically important across Bangladesh and South Asia.

diseases.name: Stem Rot
diseases.description: A severe jute disease that causes internal stem rot, plant collapse, and major yield loss under favorable conditions.
symptoms: Leaves first turn pale gray and later blacken
Internal stem tissues rot progressively
Whole plants collapse, especially in severe fields
treatments: Treat seed with Thiram or Carbendazim at 2-3 g/kg before sowing
Spray carbendazim (Bavistin 50 WP) at 1 g/L at early onset
Rotate with non-host crops and remove infected debris
Avoid prolonged waterlogging and improve drainage

diseases.name: Black Band
diseases.description: Blackish lesions on stems enlarge and girdle plants, weakening bark and reducing fiber quality.
symptoms: Blackish-brown stem patches appear and enlarge
Lesions encircle stems and form dark bands
Bark becomes dark, brittle, and damaged
treatments: Spray Mancozeb (0.25%) or copper oxychloride early
Remove and burn infected plant portions
Avoid mechanical stem injury
Maintain balanced fertilization and field drainage

diseases.name: Anthracnose
diseases.description: A stem canker disease that can girdle plants and render fibers shredded and unusable.
symptoms: Small water-soaked spots expand into sunken lesions
Large cankers girdle stems
Affected fiber becomes shredded and poor quality
treatments: Treat seed with Carbendazim at 2 g/kg
Spray Mancozeb 75 WP (2.5 g/L) or copper oxychloride (3 g/L)
Repeat sprays at 15-day intervals from symptom onset
Use disease-free seed and avoid dense sowing

diseases.name: Soft Rot
diseases.description: A collar-region stem rot with white mycelial growth that causes sudden lodging and death.
symptoms: White cottony mycelial growth near the collar
Stem rots at soil level and plants collapse
Mustard-like sclerotia appear in mycelial mats
treatments: Drench collar soil with Carbendazim (1 g/L) or Validamycin
Remove infected debris and nearby contaminated soil
Avoid excess irrigation and improve drainage
Deep plough and apply Trichoderma-based biocontrol

diseases.name: Die Back
diseases.description: A progressive top-down drying disorder that causes withering and stem death, especially in olitorius types.
symptoms: Drying begins from shoot tip and advances downward
Leaves wither and drop
Stems die back toward the base
treatments: Treat seeds with Thiram or Mancozeb at 3 g/kg
Spray Mancozeb 0.25% or Zineb 0.2% at 10-day intervals
Ensure proper field drainage
Use healthy certified seed and avoid late waterlogging

diseases.name: Wilting
diseases.description: A soil-borne wilt that causes sudden flaccidity and plant death, often severe near flowering stage.
symptoms: Leaves become flaccid and wilt suddenly
Rapid decline and plant death
More severe damage in susceptible varieties
treatments: Drench root zone with Carbendazim (0.1%) or Iprodione
Apply Trichoderma harzianum at planting
Avoid monoculture and maintain balanced soil moisture
Use resistant cultivars where available

diseases.name: Seedling Blight
diseases.description: An early-stage seedling disease causing basal lesions, collapse, and stand loss soon after germination.
symptoms: Brown to black lesions at seedling base
Seedlings collapse shortly after emergence
High mortality in wet, poorly drained conditions
treatments: Treat seed with Thiram (3 g/kg) or Captan before sowing
Apply Carbendazim (Bavistin) soil drench at germination
Avoid overly wet or compacted seedbeds
Rotate with cereals and remove dead seedlings promptly

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
diseases.description: A foliar fungal disease with white powdery growth that weakens plants and promotes defoliation.
symptoms: Fine white powder appears on upper leaf surfaces
Disease intensifies in humid or foggy weather
Leaf drop and reduced plant vigor
treatments: Spray Wettable Sulphur (2-3 g/L) or Karathane (0.5 mL/L)
Repeat spray after 10-14 days when needed
Reduce canopy density to improve airflow
Avoid excessive nitrogen and remove heavily infected leaves

diseases.name: Leaf Mosaic
diseases.pathogen: Jute Mosaic Virus (JMV); vector: whitefly (Bemisia tabaci)
diseases.description: A viral disease causing yellow mosaic mottling, leaf distortion, and growth stunting.
symptoms: Green-yellow mosaic mottling on leaves
Distorted foliage with uneven chlorophyll
Stunted growth and weak plant development
treatments: Control whiteflies with Imidacloprid 17.8 SL (0.5 mL/L) or Thiamethoxam
Rogue and destroy infected plants early
Use disease-free certified seed
Protect nurseries with fine mesh and avoid infected nearby fields

diseases.name: Leaf Curling
diseases.description: A viral curling disorder with rough, thickened leaves and reduced fiber performance.
symptoms: Leaves become pale, thick, rough, and curled
Mosaic green-yellow patterns develop
Plants become stunted with poor fiber development
treatments: Use virus-free certified seeds
Control vectors with Imidacloprid or Acetamiprid
Rogue and burn infected plants early
Incorporate infected debris after harvest and avoid seed from diseased fields

diseases.name: Root Knot
diseases.pathogen: Meloidogyne incognita, M. javanica, and M. enterolobii
diseases.description: A nematode disease causing root galls, poor nutrient uptake, stunting, and reduced fiber yield.
symptoms: Roots show swollen galls and knot formation
Plants are stunted, yellow, and unthrifty
Reduced water and nutrient uptake limits growth
treatments: Apply Carbofuran 3G (33 kg/ha) or Phorate 10G at sowing
Use neem cake (250 kg/ha) as soil amendment
Rotate with non-host crops such as cereals
Deep plough and use resistant or tolerant varieties
